turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: The second case I have is Twitter v. Taamneh. This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. In this case a group of plaintiffs have sued three social media companies; Facebook, Twitter and Google for aiding and abetting ISIS in a terrorist attack. Everyone in this case agrees on the basics of how those social media platforms operate. People from around the world signed up to the platforms and upload content to them free of charge. The platforms use recommendation algorithms to match uses with content, and both sides portrayed the algorithms here as matching anyone with any content that they are more likely to view. Plaintiffs alleged that these companies have knowingly allowed ISIS to upload content to their platforms for years. Plaintiffs also allege that ISIS has used that platform to expand its reach and recruit new terrorists. Plaintiffs accordingly sued Facebook, Twitter and Google for aiding and abetting ISIS in violation of the Justice Against Sponsors of Terrorism Act. The Ninth Circuit held that plaintiffs had stated a claim for relief. In an opinion filed with the clerk today we reverse. We hold that plaintiffs have stated a claim for aiding and abetting under the act. The phrase aiding and abetting has a long common law history. In the act here, Congress also referenced a particular D. C. Circuit decision, Halberstam v. Welch, which itself rested on the common law of aiding and abetting. Informed by this common law background the act imposes liability on those who consciously voluntarily and culpably participate in acts of terrorism committed by others. Plaintiffs here has failed to allege that defendants aided and abetted the terrorist acts at issue. Many of the plaintiff's allegations focus on the mere creation of defendants' platform and algorithms but without more these algorithms are no different from claims that cell phones or the internet to commit crimes. Other allegations focus on defendants' failure to remove or block ISIS content, but plaintiffs give no connection between defendants and ISIS beyond the same arm's length connection that defendants have with their billions of other users. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reversed the Judgement of the Ninth Circuit. The decision of the court is unanimous. Justice Jackson has filed a concurring opinion.
